pattern: Because three chosen positions always have total pairwise distance equal to twice the span between the outermost two, hunting for the tightest triple of equal values reduces to comparing only consecutive triples within each value's own sorted occurrence list.
hints: For any three chosen indices, the sum of the three pairwise absolute distances only depends on the smallest and largest of the three -- the middle index's exact position never changes the total.
So for a fixed value, the best triple of its occurrences is always three occurrences that are consecutive in that value's sorted index list, never occurrences with a gap between them.

Track each value's occurrence indices in one scan, and for every value with at least three occurrences slide a window of three consecutive indices to find its tightest span; the smallest span found across all values, doubled, is the answer.

edge_inputs.description: exactly one value, three consecutive occurrences
a repeated value with occurrences spread across the whole array
one value's triple is not physically consecutive in the array
two different values each qualify; the global answer must pick the smaller span
a value with many occurrences where only a middle consecutive window is tightest

stress.description: random values in a range tied to the array length, some values guaranteed to repeat
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 100000))
    pool = max(1, n // 4)
    nums = [rng.randint(1, pool) for _ in range(n)]
    return [nums]
stress.note: a small value pool relative to n forces many repeated values so triples are common
